summary: Gruber argues that the iPad launch event carried a meta-message about Apple's ambitions beyond the device itself. He emphasizes the iPad's remarkable speed, powered by Apple's custom-designed A4 chip, as the most striking aspect of the hands-on experience. The piece frames Apple's move into designing its own CPUs as a strategic power play, positioning the company as a vertically integrated force that controls both hardware and software. Gruber contends that Apple is asserting dominance as the strongest company in technology, combining the roles of both Microsoft and Intel in the mobile computing era.
key_points: The iPad's speed, not its screen size, was the most striking differentiator from the iPhone — web pages rendered nearly instantly and every app felt impossibly fast
Apple designing its own A4 CPU is a major strategic move, giving them ownership of the primary technology behind their mobile products
Apple's unusual willingness to publicly tout the A4 chip breaks their normal pattern of downplaying technical specs, signaling they want the industry to notice
The event's meta-message — emphasizing $50B revenue, mobile hardware leadership, and custom silicon — was Apple declaring itself the dominant force in technology
Vertical integration of hardware, software, and now CPUs may give Apple a manufacturing advantage no competitor can match at the iPad's price and performance
money_quotes: I got about 20 blessed minutes of time using the iPad demo units Apple had at the event today, and if I had to sum up the device with one word, that word would be 'fast'.
After using the iPhone so much for two and a half years, I've become accustomed to web pages rendering (relative to the Mac) slowly. On the iPad, they seem to render nearly instantly.

Part of their vision for moving computers from technical culture to popular culture is about getting away from defining these things by their technical specs. So the prominent talk about A4 is telling. This is something they want us to notice.
They're not getting into the CPU business for kicks, they're getting into it to kick ass.
They're Microsoft and Intel rolled into one when it comes to mobile computing.
Software aside (which is a huge thing to put aside), it may well be that no other company could make a device today matching the price, size, and performance of the iPad.
This is Apple's way of asserting that they're taking over the penthouse suite as the strongest and best company in the whole ones-and-zeroes racket.
key_insight: Apple's custom A4 chip in the iPad signals a strategic shift toward full vertical integration of mobile computing, positioning Apple as the only company that controls both the silicon and the software.
